The exercise is useful because it separates a biological constraint from the propulsion, navigation, weather, and logistics problems that continue after it disappears.

evidence.note: The panel considers pressure hulls, machinery, commando raids, channel crossings, and waterproof equipment.

question: How would the world wars change if humans could breathe underwater from 1900 onward?
answerShort: Submarine layouts and commando operations would change, but engines, batteries, cold, currents, and waterproof equipment would still prevent effortless underwater warfare.

title: How battleship guns might be designed today
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Modern manufacture would change automation, maintenance, and mounting choices more than the underlying physics of a very large naval gun.
body: The physical problem of building a battleship-caliber gun leaves fewer revolutionary options than the question suggests. Modern manufacturing would concentrate change in automation, maintenance access, fire control, and ammunition handling rather than overturning gun physics. The panel debates single versus twin mounts, weighing maintenance and accuracy claims against rate of fire, redundancy, and the modest footprint penalty of placing two independent guns in one turret. The discussion also shows why higher fire volume matters against swarms, for shore support, and when real combat fails to deliver the advertised one-shot kill.

evidence.note: A sustained debate covers automation and single-versus-twin mount tradeoffs.

question: If battleship guns were designed today, how would they differ?
answerShort: The guns themselves would be broadly familiar, but their mounts would use far more automation and might favor independently operating guns within simplified turrets.

title: Could old ships receive quick prewar engine overhauls?
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Regular interwar maintenance left many British and American hulls sound enough for machinery work without a complete structural rebuilding.
body: Whether a quick overhaul was realistic depended on the condition and refit history of each ship. The Royal Navy and US Navy possessed enough hulls to rotate units through regular interwar maintenance. That practice left many older ships in reasonable structural condition even when their machinery needed attention. Limited hull repairs would still accompany an engine overhaul, but a total reconstruction was not automatically required.

evidence.note: The answer ties feasibility to maintenance and refit history.

question: Could Britain or the United States quickly overhaul older ships' engines before the Second World War, or would the hulls also need major work?
answerShort: Many could receive a fairly quick engine overhaul because their hulls were regularly maintained, though some accompanying hull work would be necessary.

title: Why modernizing the Tennessee-class cruisers was unattractive
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Plans for new machinery, triple eight-inch turrets, and 27-knot speed could not overcome the cost and age of hulls that would still face newer treaty cruisers.
body: The Tennessee-class armored cruisers combined four 10-inch guns and a large six-inch battery with old vertical triple-expansion machinery and about 22 knots of speed. Historical studies considered oil firing, new power plants, closing casemates, and triple eight-inch turrets, but estimated costs rose sharply. Even a heavily rebuilt ship would remain an old hull facing weapons and designs decades newer, while reconstruction could disrupt construction of modern cruisers. A more permissive 15,000-ton treaty limit might keep them in service, yet it would also produce much stronger British, Japanese, and Italian cruisers that made the Tennessees even less competitive. Converting them to escort carriers is physically imaginable but probably less economical than building purpose-designed aviation ships.

evidence.note: The panel considers machinery, armament, carrier conversion, historical studies, costs, treaty limits, and competing cruisers.

question: What if the Tennessee-class armored cruisers were modernized in 1928?
answerShort: They could be rebuilt extensively, but cost, age, treaty politics, and the superiority of new cruisers make the investment hard to justify.

title: Why CV90 is not a simple Bradley replacement
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: CV90 is mature and capable, but its age, troop layout, armament limits, and mismatch with current American requirements argue against adopting it unchanged.
body: The panel separates the vehicle's quality from its suitability for a specific procurement competition. CV90 is a mature and adaptable platform, but its age and troop arrangement do not make it a direct answer to every American requirement. The US Army wants a vehicle organized around its established infantry component and a 50 mm weapon. Newer candidate designs and domestic industrial arrangements fit those demands more directly, so NATO standardization alone does not decide the competition.

evidence.note: The panel discusses design age, troop capacity, armament, and US requirements.

question: Why did the US Army not adopt CV90 as the Bradley replacement after Sweden joined NATO?
answerShort: CV90 is a good but aging platform that does not align closely enough with the US Army's current troop, weapon, and procurement requirements.

title: Japanese options if the Pacific Fleet stayed on the US West Coast
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Without the fleet at Pearl Harbor, Japan would probably shift attention toward the Philippines rather than attempt an unsustainable mass-carrier raid on San Diego.
body: The distance to the American West Coast would make supporting a large carrier striking force extremely difficult. Japan lacked the replenishment system needed to keep the Pearl Harbor attack force off San Diego. A token raid or a specially developed long-range aviation-submarine operation might be conceivable, but neither would replace the historical carrier strike. The Philippines offered a more practical operational target and fit Japan's wider campaign requirements.

evidence.note: The answer compares Pearl Harbor, the Philippines, and the logistics of reaching San Diego.

question: How would Japanese plans change if the United States had not moved the Pacific Fleet to Pearl Harbor?
answerShort: Japan probably would not attack Pearl Harbor and would more likely concentrate on the Philippines, since a major strike on San Diego was logistically unrealistic.

title: How machinery layout could make high-pressure steam reliable
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Reliability would depend on short, simple steam runs and modular boiler-turbine-generator rooms rather than copying fragile German arrangements.
body: The answer separates high steam pressure from the particular engineering choices that made some plants troublesome. Joints, bends, and long pipe runs create more difficulty than straight high-pressure tubing. A British design might therefore pair each boiler closely with its turbine and generator, limiting pipe length while creating several independent machinery units. That arrangement could improve damage tolerance, allow maintenance while other units supplied the ship, and exploit high-pressure steam through electric drive.

evidence.note: The answer links steam reliability to piping geometry and modular electric-drive machinery rooms.

question: What reliability and maintenance problems would accompany advanced high-pressure naval machinery?
answerShort: The main risks lie in joints and complex pipe runs, so compact modular boiler-turbine-generator units could make the plant more reliable and maintainable.

title: Britain's planned role in the invasion of Japan
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: British forces were already being shifted from Europe toward the Pacific and would have joined the planned invasion of Japan.
body: The Allies ended the European war with an unprecedented concentration of shipping, landing craft, and logistical organizations. Moving British formations east would still take months, but the transport capacity and institutional experience existed. The limiting question was political will and scheduling rather than whether the movement could be organized at all. The answer emphasizes that British participation was an active preparation, not a retrospective possibility.

evidence.note: The panel confirms British participation and describes the available Allied movement capacity.

question: Were British forces and resources being moved from Europe for the planned invasion of Japan?
answerShort: Yes; British troops were intended to participate, and the Allied transport system could move them east given several months.

title: Why Singapore's fall divided the Allied naval war
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: A surviving Singapore base could have supported a more integrated Anglo-American Pacific war, while its loss split British effort toward the Indian Ocean and Southeast Asia.
body: The answer treats basing geography as the reason cooperation developed unevenly. Once Singapore fell, Britain had to sustain major campaigns across the Indian Ocean, Burma, and Southeast Asia while the United States concentrated on the central and southwest Pacific. Those campaigns consumed Japanese forces and affected the wider naval balance even when they disappeared from American narratives of the war. A functioning Singapore would have provided the physical hub for a more visibly joint theater.

evidence.note: The answer connects Singapore's loss with the division between Pacific and Indian Ocean campaigns.

question: Could the Pacific have operated as a more joint British-American theater from the beginning of the Second World War?
answerShort: Yes if Singapore remained available; after its loss, British operations centered on the Indian Ocean and Southeast Asia while the US drove across the Pacific.

title: Which admirals would be worst at organizing basic sanitation?
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Admiral Aube and Richmond Kelly Turner are offered as leaders whose disruptive policies or temperament make them risky choices for even a basic organizational task.
body: The deliberately comic question becomes a comparison of damaging senior leadership styles. Admiral Aube is selected because his Jeune École policies interrupted French capital-ship construction and produced a confused fleet program. Richmond Kelly Turner is offered as an American alternative because of his difficult personality and his role in pre-Pearl Harbor intelligence failures, despite later success in amphibious command. The answer distinguishes technical competence from the judgment and temperament needed to organize people effectively.

evidence.note: The panel compares Admiral Aube's fleet policy with Richmond Kelly Turner's temperament and mixed record.

question: Which historical admiral would be the worst choice to organize a latrine-digging party?
answerShort: Admiral Aube is the leading choice for his disruptive Jeune École program, with Richmond Kelly Turner offered as a comparably difficult American candidate.

title: Why Russia still needs the Project 23900 amphibious ships
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: The ships answer a genuine Russian amphibious shortfall, but their value depends on escorts, machinery supply, and whether the long-delayed hulls actually enter service.
body: The failed effort to land near Odesa highlighted the absence of large aviation-capable amphibious ships in Russia's order of battle. Project 23900 could carry helicopters, landing forces, and command facilities useful in the Black Sea and other theaters. The ships would also force NATO planners to account for possible operations against northern Norway or Baltic positions. Their slow construction, uncertain machinery sources, limited escort pool, and allocation between fleets sharply constrain that theoretical value.

evidence.note: The panel covers amphibious need, strategic dilemmas, escorts, construction, and machinery.

question: Are the Project 23900 amphibious ships a poor investment given the state of Russia's surface fleet?
answerShort: No; they fill a real amphibious gap, but construction risk and limited escorts may prevent Russia from realizing their full operational value.

title: How to make a fictional military force internally consistent
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: A convincing fictional force needs equipment, organization, uniforms, logistics, and tactics that follow from a coherent mission and adapt to local conditions.
body: The panel starts with the force's job, environment, and available resources rather than with a list of attractive weapons. Procurement, uniforms, logistics, and doctrine should all support the same intended way of fighting. Real forces also adapt imperfectly, so variation between theaters can be more credible than rigid uniformity. Byzantine cavalry changing equipment and tactics for campaigning in Bulgaria illustrates how terrain and opponents should reshape a military system.

evidence.note: The answer links force design to mission, context, equipment, and adaptation, using Byzantine cavalry as an example.

question: What makes the military forces in a fictional setting feel consistent?
answerShort: Their equipment, organization, logistics, and tactics should arise from a shared mission and then adapt plausibly to different environments and enemies.

title: Why high-pressure boilers would not refit the whole Royal Navy
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: High-pressure plants would make sense for new fleet destroyers, cruisers, carriers, and capital ships, but not for every escort and older conversion.
body: The expense and disruption of opening existing ships would be justified only where the extra power improved the mission. Fleet destroyers, cruisers, carriers, and capital ships could exploit greater machinery density and speed. Slow escorts, corvettes, and older destroyer conversions did not need the same performance because their missions emphasized endurance and convoy work. A selective program would therefore put the new boilers into high-value fast units instead of treating one technology as a universal refit.

evidence.note: The answer identifies which ship types would and would not benefit.

question: Could Britain replace all naval boilers with high-pressure plants before the Second World War?
answerShort: No, and it would not need to; the technology belonged in new high-performance fleet units, not every escort or older ship.

title: Fletcher machinery offers little to a Canadian Tribal
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: A Fletcher power plant could be fitted in theory, but modest speed gains would not compensate for redesign and an isolated American spare-parts chain.
body: The machinery would raise installed power from roughly 44,000 to 60,000 shaft horsepower. Similar displacement and hull limits mean that large increase buys only a small rise from roughly 36 knots toward 37 or 38 knots. Canadian Tribals operating with British forces would also lack convenient spares for an American plant. The substitution is technically possible, but its logistics burden and marginal operational gain give designers little reason to adopt it.

evidence.note: The panel compares horsepower, speed, hull form, and spare-parts support.

question: Could Canada build a Tribal-class destroyer with Fletcher-class machinery, and would it be useful?
answerShort: It was technically possible but operationally unattractive because the speed gain was small and the American machinery complicated support.

title: Why Russia might pair nuclear power with STOBAR aviation
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: A Russian successor to Kuznetsov could plausibly combine nuclear propulsion with a familiar ski-jump aviation system to maximize prestige while limiting technological change.
body: The panel treats a nuclear-powered STOBAR carrier as politically and institutionally plausible even if it is not the most efficient design. Nuclear propulsion would advertise national status and give the project a claim to technological advance. Retaining ski-jump operations would preserve experience with an existing aviation model and avoid the additional risk of developing a full catapult system at the same time. The combination reflects how prestige, industrial confidence, and incremental change can shape procurement more strongly than a clean-sheet operational optimum.

evidence.note: The panel weighs nuclear prestige against the familiarity and lower development risk of continued STOBAR operations.

title: Could the RAF return to heavy bombers?
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: B-21 is the only accessible heavy-bomber program, but cost and export access are serious obstacles despite a plausible NATO and maritime-strike role.
body: The answer argues that Britain can contribute most effectively to NATO through high-end naval and air capabilities rather than attempting to match continental mass armies. A small bomber force could support global strike and maritime missions while British staffs continued to provide divisional-level command expertise. B-21 is the only current heavy-bomber program that broadly fits, but the RAF is unlikely to receive the necessary budget and export approval remains uncertain. An Anglo-French design might return some work to British industry, yet repeated disputes over leadership and work share make that route slow and risky. AUKUS gives Britain and Australia unusually close access to American technology, which makes an eventual off-the-shelf B-21 purchase more plausible than it would be for most allies. The exchange frames bomber procurement as a trade among alliance specialization, sovereign industry, time, and cost rather than as an aircraft-performance question alone.

evidence.note: The panel considers NATO specialization, maritime strike, B-21 access, AUKUS, budget, and joint-program industrial tradeoffs.

question: Should the Royal Air Force seek a successor to the Vulcan?
answerShort: A small B-21-like force could be strategically useful, especially for maritime strike, but access and affordability make it improbable.

title: Why the War of 1812 resists a simple winner
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Britain and the United States entered the War of 1812 with different priorities, so its outcome depends on which war aims and willingness to continue are being measured.
body: The panel cautions against reducing the War of 1812 to a single scoreboard. Britain treated North America as a secondary theater while fighting Napoleon, whereas the United States pursued grievances and territorial aims closer to home. Neither side achieved every objective, and the eventual settlement reflected the cost and political value of continuing. The useful question is therefore which aims survived the war, not simply which side won more engagements.

evidence.note: The panel frames the outcome around different war aims and each side's willingness to continue.

title: Why diplomatic failure is not always an ambassador's failure
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: An apparently failed ambassador may have reported a crisis accurately while ministers at home ignored or discounted the warning.
body: The panel resists naming a worst British ambassador without first separating reporting from policy. Diplomats can transmit accurate warnings yet lack the authority to make ministers act on them. The lead-up to the Falklands War is offered as a case where signals from Argentina could be assessed correctly but mishandled in London. Judging diplomacy therefore requires tracing what information reached the government and where the decision process actually failed.

evidence.note: The answer distinguishes diplomatic reporting from ministerial decisions and references the Falklands crisis.

question: Who was the worst British ambassador in modern history?
answerShort: That cannot be judged from the outcome alone, because an ambassador may have warned accurately while ministers ignored the report; the Falklands crisis illustrates the distinction.

title: How Napoleon's defeat changed the War of 1812
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Napoleon's first defeat freed British ships and veteran troops for North America, sharply changing the balance without making the theater Britain's highest priority.
body: For most of the war Britain had to devote its strongest attention to Napoleon. His first defeat released veteran formations and additional naval power for service in North America. That reinforcement increased pressure on the United States and helped produce operations such as the advance on Washington. The shift demonstrates how the outcome of a secondary theater can depend on strategic events far outside it.

evidence.note: The panel explains that Napoleon's defeat released British veterans and naval forces for North America.

title: What Constellation and carrier delays reveal
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Delays do not prove that American shipbuilding is finished, but they compound cost, schedule, and industrial-capacity problems.
body: The panel treats delays to the frigate and carrier programs as serious symptoms rather than evidence of total collapse. Schedule slippage postpones needed capability and compounds pressure elsewhere in the fleet. Electronics-heavy warships are also exposed to material-price and industrial-policy shocks, including changes in the cost of copper. The useful warning is that shipbuilding performance depends on supply chains and fiscal policy as well as activity inside the yard.

evidence.note: The answer discusses schedule trouble, costs, and the industrial base.

question: What do delays to the Constellation frigate and John F. Kennedy say about US shipbuilding?
answerShort: The industry is not beyond recovery, but the delays create major cost and force-planning problems and expose deeper industrial weaknesses.

title: The young United States' institutional limits in 1812
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: The United States possessed resources and manpower in 1812 but lacked the mature fiscal, administrative, and military systems needed to mobilize them efficiently.
body: The panel separates national potential from the ability to turn it into wartime power. The young federal government had limited revenue, administrative reach, trained forces, and logistical machinery. Those weaknesses help explain uneven American operations despite the country's size and local advantages. The war became an early test of state capacity as much as a contest of battlefield skill.

evidence.note: The panel contrasts American resources with the immature institutions available to mobilize them.

title: Why a future naval gun may jump from five to six inches
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: If navies move beyond five-inch guns, modern rates of fire and shell volume make a six-inch weapon more attractive than reviving 5.25-inch caliber.
body: Modern automation can give a six-inch gun a rate of fire approaching smaller weapons. Its larger projectile also carries disproportionately more payload for guided, proximity-fused, or other specialized ammunition. The panel therefore sees a direct move to six inches as more plausible for layered defense and surface missions than a return to the intermediate British 5.25-inch caliber. The answer distinguishes the caliber choices of naval guns from the different barrel-length and mobility constraints that shape modern tank armament.

evidence.note: The panel compares 5.25-inch, 130 mm, and six-inch weapons.

question: Could layered air defense bring back the 5.25-inch naval gun?
answerShort: Probably not; if navies go larger than five inches, a modern six-inch gun offers a more compelling payload advantage.

title: The minimum crew needed to move a Tribal-class destroyer
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: A handful of experts might move a Tribal-class destroyer in an emergency, but even basic propulsion and navigation divide attention across too many essential stations for safe normal operation.
body: The panel treats the question as emergency movement rather than combat readiness. One boiler and one engine room could be operated while separate people handled steering, navigation, lookout, and communications. Four people might just move the ship in an extreme improvisation, but roughly ten is suggested as a more credible absolute minimum. Neither figure provides damage control, sustained watches, weapons operation, or the redundancy expected for a safe Atlantic passage.

evidence.note: The panel allocates propulsion, steering, and navigation roles and distinguishes an extreme four-person move from a roughly ten-person minimum.

question: What is the smallest crew that could take a Tribal-class destroyer across the Atlantic?
answerShort: Perhaps four in a desperate improvisation, but about ten specialists is a more credible bare minimum—and still far short of a safe operational crew.

title: Would Hood have received a West Virginia-style rebuild?
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Hood's different damage and continuing value as a fast capital ship would have produced a repair program unlike West Virginia's reconstruction.
body: The panel rejects a direct copy of West Virginia's rebuilding path. Hood would have suffered a different damage pattern and therefore would not begin with the same structural problem. Her speed also made her more valuable as a mobile fleet unit than an older American standard battleship. A Norfolk repair would likely restore and modernize Hood around that fast-capital-ship role rather than transform her into a West Virginia analogue.

evidence.note: The answer emphasizes different damage and Hood's continued value as a fast unit.

question: If Hood survived Denmark Strait and reached Norfolk, would she be rebuilt like USS West Virginia?
answerShort: No; her different damage and much higher speed would drive a distinct repair and modernization program.

title: Type 45 and Burke defenses work best together
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Type 45's radar offers useful ballistic-missile tracking qualities, but full interception needs the appropriate Aster variant and effective data sharing with allied ships.
body: The panel rejects a simple claim that either Type 45 or an Arleigh Burke is categorically superior. SAMPSON and Aegis bring different strengths to detecting and engaging ballistic targets. A Type 45 needs the correct Aster missile variant for the full mission, while a Burke contributes its own sensors and SM-series weapons. The strongest defensive arrangement combines both ships and shares their tracks, a capability weakened when interoperability equipment is deferred.

evidence.note: The answer compares SAMPSON, Aegis, Aster, SM-6, and cooperative data sharing.

question: Would a Type 45 intercept ballistic missiles in the Red Sea more efficiently than an Arleigh Burke using SM-6?
answerShort: Not by itself; the best defense combines Type 45 and Burke sensors and weapons, provided they can exchange data effectively.

title: What a more efficient Bismarck redesign might change
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: A cleaner Bismarck design might use three triple main turrets and a more unified secondary battery, though the panel reserves a full redesign for a dedicated analysis.
body: The panel avoids pretending that a complete battleship redesign can be solved in a brief live answer. It nevertheless identifies the four twin main turrets as an inefficient use of length and protected volume compared with three triples. A unified 105 mm secondary battery could also simplify the mixed intermediate and anti-aircraft armament. Those changes would release weight and internal space, but the armor scheme, machinery, stability, and doctrine would still require an integrated design study.

evidence.note: The panel proposes three triple turrets and a unified 105 mm battery while deferring a complete redesign.

question: How would the panel redesign Bismarck while keeping the same displacement?
answerShort: Three triple main turrets and a unified 105 mm secondary battery are plausible first changes, but a responsible answer needs a full design analysis.

title: How 850 psi machinery would change the Nelson design
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Very high-pressure machinery could move the Nelson design toward the faster F3 concept, although British experience in 1921 was not mature enough to make that an easy choice.
body: The panel links the question to the faster F3 battlecruiser concept rather than merely substituting boilers into the completed Nelson hull. Greater power density could support a ship around 28 knots while preserving heavy armament and protection. Britain had tested high-pressure marine boilers before the First World War, so the idea was not wholly unknown. The obstacle was turning experimental experience into a reliable fleet installation at the required scale and date.

evidence.note: The answer connects 850 psi machinery with the F3 concept, roughly 28 knots, and early British boiler trials.

question: What would the Nelson-class design look like with 850 psi boilers?
answerShort: It would resemble the faster F3 concept, potentially reaching about 28 knots, but British high-pressure machinery was not yet mature enough for a risk-free 1921 design.

title: What a 1942 engine could—and could not—do for Swordfish
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: A late engine swap would bring limited gains to the draggy Swordfish airframe, while an equivalent engine available during design would probably produce a different monoplane aircraft.
body: The panel distinguishes retrofitting an existing Swordfish from redesigning the aircraft around a more powerful engine. Added power in 1942 would improve performance only modestly because the biplane airframe imposed large aerodynamic limits. If the same engine had been available in the early 1930s, Fairey would probably have designed a monoplane rather than a faster Swordfish. The discussion also notes that simply carrying a heavier 21-inch weapon would not solve torpedo delivery, because an air-dropped torpedo had to survive water entry and follow a stable run.

evidence.note: The panel separates a late engine retrofit from an early clean-sheet design and discusses torpedo water-entry limits.

question: How would the Swordfish change if it received a good 1942 engine?
answerShort: A retrofit would help only modestly, but an early design built around that power would likely become a different monoplane; heavier torpedoes would still require suitable air-drop engineering.

title: How low-flying Buccaneers were lost to the sea
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Sea-skimming Buccaneer training could bring aircraft into contact with waves, causing serious structural or engine damage even when the exercise profile looked survivable on paper.
body: The panel recalls more than one exercise incident involving Buccaneers flown at extreme low level over water. One aircraft was badly damaged after striking a wave, while another suffered water ingestion and the crew had to eject. Exercise sensors could even record an aircraft as submerged when it passed below the modeled sea surface. The examples show the gap between the theoretical advantages of terrain masking and the unforgiving physical margin of real sea-skimming flight.

evidence.note: The panel recounts wave strikes, water ingestion, an ejection, and exercise sensors treating a very low aircraft as submerged.

question: Was a Buccaneer ever knocked down by a wave during an exercise?
answerShort: Yes; the panel recalls serious wave-strike incidents, including water ingestion that forced an ejection, illustrating the risks of true sea-skimming flight.

title: The operational difference between landing craft and landing ships
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: The distinction rests on operational scope: craft conduct the final ship-to-shore movement, while ships transport forces between theaters and may carry craft of their own.
body: A landing craft is optimized for the short movement between a transport or nearby coast and the beach. Larger craft may also make limited shore-to-shore movements. A landing ship carries troops, vehicles, and supplies across strategic distance before conducting or supporting the assault. It may beach itself or launch smaller craft, so size alone does not define the category as clearly as the vessel's role in the operation.

evidence.note: The answer defines craft and ships by their roles and operating scope.

question: When does a vessel carrying infantry become a landing ship rather than a landing craft or boat?
answerShort: When its role expands from the local ship-to-shore movement to strategic transport and support of the landing operation.

title: Why naval alcohol rations did not automatically create alcoholism
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Traditional rum or beer issues were controlled, calorie-bearing rations rather than unlimited drinking, though wider alcohol abuse could still produce dependence and malnutrition.
body: The panel distinguishes a measured shipboard ration from unrestricted alcohol consumption. Beer and spirits supplied calories, morale, and historically a safer stored drink than questionable water. The issued amount was too small by itself to explain broad alcohol dependence among sailors. Abuse outside the ration system remained dangerous, however, and heavy drinking could replace food calories and cause serious nutritional harm.

evidence.note: The panel contrasts the measured ration with alcohol abuse and discusses calories, water quality, dependence, and nutrition.

question: Did beer and rum rations make sailors alcoholic?
answerShort: Not by themselves; the controlled issue was small and served practical purposes, although additional heavy drinking could still cause addiction and malnutrition.

title: Why a carrier F-104 would require a near-total redesign
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: The F-104's landing gear, structure, approach behavior, and carrier compatibility would need extensive changes, making a naval version feasible only as a substantially new aircraft for larger later carriers.
body: Carrier operation would demand stronger and differently placed landing gear, an arresting system, corrosion protection, and structure able to absorb repeated deck landings. Those additions would raise weight and disturb an airframe already optimized around a narrow high-speed mission. Its approach and low-speed handling would also be poor matches for early postwar carrier decks. By the time a Phantom-capable carrier could operate such a design, the resulting naval Starfighter would be so extensively altered that other aircraft would offer a better starting point.

evidence.note: The panel discusses landing gear, structural weight, deck compatibility, approach performance, and the scale of redesign required.

question: Could the F-104 Starfighter be navalized for carrier service?
answerShort: Only through major structural and handling changes that would produce an effectively new aircraft, and it would need a later, larger carrier to be practical.

title: Was Salamis the ancient world's most important battle?
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Salamis was important, but Greek and Roman source survival magnifies its apparent uniqueness and obscures Persian influence on later Greek politics and war.
body: The panel accepts Salamis as a major battle without treating it as the single decisive hinge of the ancient world. Surviving Greek and Roman narratives naturally center the conflicts that mattered to their own traditions. Persian records survive far less completely, making Persian power and policy easier to understate. Persia continued to influence Greek affairs afterward, including through financial support that helped Sparta defeat Athens in the Peloponnesian War.

evidence.note: The panel qualifies Salamis's importance through source bias and Persia's later role in Greek affairs.

question: Was the Battle of Salamis the most monumental event of the ancient world?
answerShort: It was highly important, but its exceptional status is amplified by Greek and Roman source survival and by the loss of much of the Persian perspective.

title: What would it take for a Malta-class carrier to be built?
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Completing a Malta-class carrier required a different British political decision at the end of the Second World War, when postwar priorities outweighed the ship's potential value.
body: The obstacle was not a missing technical concept but the government's end-of-war decision about what Britain could afford. A Malta-class carrier could have given the postwar Royal Navy a larger and more adaptable aviation platform. Building one would therefore require ministers to protect the program as wartime orders contracted. The short answer points readers toward the wider carrier-policy discussion rather than inventing a narrow engineering trigger.

evidence.note: The answer identifies a different end-of-war government decision as the necessary change and notes the carrier's potential value.

question: What would need to change for at least one Malta-class carrier to be built?
answerShort: The British government would have to make a different postwar priority decision and preserve the program despite demobilization and financial pressure.

title: Could a Blackburn Blackburn carry anti-submarine rockets?
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: A Blackburn Blackburn could carry rocket rails only if an extreme equipment shortage kept the obsolete aircraft in service until wartime RP-3 weapons became available.
body: The panel first corrects the premise: British aircraft generally carried individual rockets on rails rather than modern rocket pods. Effective RP-3 armament did not appear until the middle of the Second World War, long after the Blackburn Blackburn should have disappeared from frontline use. An aircraft surviving through an extraordinary procurement crisis might be adapted for anti-submarine work because submarines imposed modest speed requirements. That possibility is technically imaginable but operationally unattractive, and the panel finds no good reason to prefer it over newer aircraft.

evidence.note: The panel corrects pods to rails, dates RP-3 availability, and considers an emergency ASW adaptation.

question: What would it take to equip the Blackburn Blackburn with anti-submarine rocket pods?
answerShort: It would require the obsolete aircraft to survive into the midwar RP-3 era, and even then it would carry rails rather than pods and remain a poor choice.

title: Defeating the RAF would not solve Operation Sea Lion
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Any Sea Lion scenario must define what defeating the RAF means and still confront the Royal Navy's ability to intervene at night.
body: Temporary local air superiority is not the same as destroying Britain's ability to build, deploy, and replace aircraft. German planning focused on Fighter Command south of London and did not account adequately for RAF groups in the Midlands and north. British fighters could withdraw beyond effective German range while still returning to contest an invasion. Even after granting a German air victory, the crossing would remain vulnerable at night, when Royal Navy battleships, cruisers, and destroyers could accept heavy losses to destroy the barges and isolate troops already ashore.

evidence.note: The answer challenges the RAF-defeat premise, details Fighter Command depth, and explains Royal Navy night action.

question: How could Germany have carried out Operation Sea Lion if the Luftwaffe defeated the RAF?
answerShort: The premise is insufficient: Germany would need a durable definition of RAF defeat and still had no convincing answer to Royal Navy intervention, especially at night.

title: Why EJ200 engines would turn Buccaneer into a new aircraft
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Modern EJ200 engines could transform Buccaneer performance, but integrating them would alter weight, balance, structure, and timing so extensively that the result would be a new design arriving after its replacement.
body: The compact EJ200 would provide far more thrust than the Buccaneer's Spey engines, but propulsion cannot be exchanged in isolation. Different engine mass and dimensions would shift the center of gravity and require new intakes, mounts, systems, and structural reinforcement. The wing roots and aging airframes would also need serious work to exploit the extra performance safely. Because EJ200 development matured around the time Buccaneer retired and Tornado already filled its mission, the project would become an expensive Super Buccaneer rather than a useful service-life extension.

evidence.note: The panel discusses thrust, engine mass, center of gravity, wing fatigue, development timing, and the resulting Super Buccaneer concept.

question: How would the Buccaneer perform with EJ200 engines?
answerShort: Performance could be dramatic, but the integration and structural changes would create an effectively new aircraft too late to compete with Tornado.

title: The Buccaneer's abandoned torpedo option
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Torpedo armament appeared in early Buccaneer thinking but was considered and rejected before service.
body: The aircraft's early anti-shipping concepts still inherited the possibility of aerial torpedo attack. Designers therefore considered torpedoes among the Buccaneer's possible weapons. The Royal Navy examined the option but chose not to carry it forward into the operational fit. The short answer is useful because it separates a documented design consideration from a weapon the aircraft actually carried in service.

evidence.note: The panel confirms early consideration and rejection.

question: Was the Blackburn Buccaneer ever considered for anti-shipping torpedoes?
answerShort: Yes, torpedoes appeared in early concepts, but the idea was rejected.

title: If Bismarck exploded immediately after sinking Hood
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: An immediate Bismarck loss would leave Prinz Eugen exposed to Prince of Wales and the pursuing cruisers while destroying Germany's chance to turn Hood's sinking into a propaganda victory.
body: The scenario reverses the emotional and operational meaning of Denmark Strait. Bismarck would still have destroyed the famous Hood, but her own sudden explosion would make the exchange look like mutual destruction rather than a German triumph. Prinz Eugen would then face Prince of Wales and nearby British cruisers without the battleship that shielded her escape. Germany would lose both its modern capital ship and much of the propaganda value gained from defeating an older First World War-era battlecruiser.

evidence.note: The panel assesses Prinz Eugen's exposure, British follow-on forces, and the changed propaganda effect.

question: What happens if Bismarck's boiler rooms explode just after Hood sinks?
answerShort: Prinz Eugen is likely overwhelmed by the remaining British ships, and Germany loses both Bismarck and the propaganda benefit of Hood's destruction.

title: How Western Anatolia would reshape Greek naval planning
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Holding Western Anatolia would push Greece toward destroyers, submarines, and coastal artillery suited to defending the Aegean littoral.
body: Western Anatolia would lengthen the Greek-held Aegean littoral and multiply the waters that had to be watched. The geography favors numerous smaller combatants able to patrol constrained seas and threaten enemy movement. Submarines and destroyers would reinforce a defensive network supported by shore-based artillery. Procurement would therefore emphasize distributed coastal denial rather than demand a wholly new battle fleet.

evidence.note: The answer identifies destroyers, submarines, and artillery.

question: How would a Greek victory in the Greco-Turkish War and control of Western Anatolia affect naval procurement?
answerShort: Greece would probably buy more destroyers and submarines and strengthen coastal artillery for a defensive Aegean strategy.

title: What a country needs to build competitive ferries
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Competitive ferry construction depends on sustained commercial orders, specialized yards, skilled supply chains, and market scale rather than on naval shipbuilding capacity alone.
body: The panel treats ferry construction as a specialized commercial ecosystem. A country needs yards that repeatedly build the type, suppliers familiar with its systems, and enough orders to keep skills and facilities active. Britain can still produce selected commercial vessels, but isolated naval or tanker work does not automatically recreate a competitive ferry sector. Finland, the Netherlands, China, and Japan illustrate how particular shipbuilding niches persist where industry and demand reinforce each other.

evidence.note: The panel compares commercial shipbuilding niches and explains the need for continuing capacity, skills, and orders.

question: What would it take for a country to develop a competitive ferry-building industry?
answerShort: Sustained commercial demand, specialized yards, trained suppliers, and repeated production are essential; occasional naval construction is not enough.

title: How a surviving Lord Nelson class might serve today
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: A modernized Lord Nelson class would combine automated heavy guns, new propulsion and sensors, and modern defensive weapons for fire support and visible regional presence.
body: Keeping the pre-dreadnoughts operational would require replacement machinery, modern electronics, and highly automated 12-inch and 9.2-inch gun systems. Missiles and close-in weapons would be added because gun power alone would not make the ships survivable in a modern threat environment. Their strongest role would be shore bombardment and conspicuous presence rather than front-line fleet combat. The panel imagines them supporting operations in confined theaters such as the Gulf or Baltic, where heavy guns could be useful but political and protection demands would remain substantial.

evidence.note: The panel outlines automated heavy guns, replacement systems, defensive weapons, and likely Gulf or Baltic uses.

question: What would the Lord Nelson class look like if it survived and was fully modernized?
answerShort: It would need new machinery, sensors, automation, missiles, and close-in defenses, becoming a specialized fire-support and presence ship rather than a modern battleship.

title: If Britain's 1915 battleships became 1939 Lion-class ships
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Replacing Britain's 1915 capital ships with fast, radar-equipped Lion-class designs would leave the High Seas Fleet overwhelmingly outclassed in range, speed, firepower, and detection.
body: The hypothetical gives Britain ships around 30 knots with nine 16-inch guns, modern protection, and radar against a First World War opponent. Even one such squadron would combine battlecruiser speed with battleship power and far better fire control. The existing Fifth Battle Squadron already posed a serious threat at Jutland; replacing it and other units with Lions would make German tactical escape far harder. The answer therefore predicts a radically one-sided naval balance rather than a modest British advantage.

evidence.note: The panel compares Lion speed, guns, radar, and protection with the High Seas Fleet and the historical Fifth Battle Squadron.

question: What if every Royal Navy battleship present in 1915 became a 1939 Lion-class battleship?
answerShort: The High Seas Fleet would be comprehensively outclassed by faster, heavier-gunned, radar-equipped ships, making any major fleet action overwhelmingly favorable to Britain.

title: If every Royal Navy aircraft became a Fairey Gannet in 1939
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: An all-Gannet Fleet Air Arm would lack a true fighter but gain exceptional range, payload, sensors, and anti-submarine capability, reshaping Norway, Taranto, and the U-boat war.
body: British carriers would need stronger arresting arrangements and crews trained for an aircraft decades ahead of their normal equipment. The Gannet would be a poor fighter, so Britain would still seek Fulmars or another dedicated interceptor. As a strike, reconnaissance, radar, and anti-submarine platform, however, it would give the fleet extraordinary range, endurance, payload, and detection capability in 1939. The panel expects much heavier pressure on U-boats, a more difficult German invasion of Norway, and an even more destructive attack on Taranto.

evidence.note: The panel covers carrier modifications, the fighter gap, ASW and radar advantages, Norway, U-boats, and Taranto.

question: What if every Royal Navy aircraft turned into a Fairey Gannet in June 1939?
answerShort: Britain would still need fighters, but Gannet range, sensors, payload, and ASW performance could transform the U-boat war, Norway, and Taranto.

title: What the US Navy could do after cancelling Constellation
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Cancellation would reopen the escort gap and leave an unmodified foreign design such as Type 26 as the fastest credible fallback.
body: The Navy needs an escort with serious anti-submarine capability, and Constellation is the nearest program to supplying it. Cancellation would reopen the requirement after years of design and acquisition work. Restarting that process would consume more time while the escort gap continued to grow. An emergency alternative would therefore require copying a proven ship such as Type 26 with minimal American redesign, because another heavily modified foreign design would repeat the delay.

evidence.note: The answer describes the escort requirement and Type 26 fallback.

question: What does the US Navy do if the Constellation class is cancelled?
answerShort: It faces a severe escort gap and may have to adopt an existing ASW design such as Type 26 almost unchanged to recover time.

title: Why Deltic diesels are a poor fit for an ekranoplan
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Napier Deltics provide too little power and dislike the changing loads of flight, making them a poor basis for a large high-performance ground-effect vehicle.
body: The Deltic was a compact opposed-piston diesel suited to marine and locomotive applications, but each engine produced only a few thousand horsepower. A useful ekranoplan needs enormous installed power to accelerate onto the cushion and remain controllable close to the surface. Flight also imposes variable and sometimes negative-g loads unlike the steadier environment for which the Deltic was designed. The panel therefore regards the project as an expensive diversion that would consume British funding without yielding a competitive vehicle.

evidence.note: The panel explains Deltic output, opposed-piston construction, variable flight loads, ekranoplan power demands, and funding consequences.

question: What if Britain built a Deltic-powered ekranoplan?
answerShort: It would be underpowered and mechanically ill-suited to flight loads, turning into a costly technology program with little operational payoff.

title: If the United States adopted Lancaster instead of its wartime bombers
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: American Lancaster adoption could increase bomb load and reduce some losses, but it requires an implausibly early British engine and airframe path and would not immediately overturn US daylight-bombing doctrine.
body: The scenario first requires the Manchester and its engines to mature early enough for Britain to offer a reliable Lancaster design before American types are entrenched. Washington would also need a reason to license a foreign bomber and adapt factories, logistics, and doctrine around it. Lancaster payload and range could improve the efficiency of some missions and might reduce losses by requiring fewer aircraft for a given bomb weight. American commanders would still begin with a commitment to daylight precision bombing, so the aircraft change alone would not erase the doctrinal learning that produced escort fighters and revised formations.

evidence.note: The panel examines engine and airframe timing, British willingness to sell, American production, bomb load, losses, and daylight doctrine.

question: What if the United States built Lancaster bombers instead of the B-17, B-24, and B-25?
answerShort: The Lancaster could deliver more bomb load and perhaps reduce losses, but obtaining it early is difficult and American daylight doctrine would still shape how it fought.

title: If US Standard battleships became Vermont-style giants in 1916
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Vermont-scale Standards with twelve 18-inch guns would strain the Panama Canal, force a two-ocean basing problem, and trigger an unaffordable international battleship race.
body: The imagined ships are too broad for the existing Panama Canal and too costly to treat as ordinary replacements. The United States would have to widen the canal, accept separate Atlantic and Pacific battle fleets, or redesign the ships around the transit limit. Britain and Japan would answer with larger projects of their own, including escalations beyond their historical late-war programs. The financial and diplomatic shock would likely destroy the treaty system before it could stabilize the competition.

evidence.note: The panel discusses beam, the Panama Canal, two-ocean basing, British and Japanese responses, cost, and treaty failure.

question: What if every US Standard-type battleship became a Vermont-style design with twelve 18-inch guns in 1916?
answerShort: The ships would create a canal and basing crisis and provoke an extreme international arms race that the major powers could scarcely afford.

title: How sailing ships survived lightning strikes
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Wooden ships initially relied on the strike finding a path into the sea, while later conductors offered a safer route around explosive damage to wet masts.
body: Lightning could flash-boil moisture inside a wooden mast and burst it apart, turning the rig into a source of splinters and wreckage. A strike might otherwise travel down the mast or along wet exterior surfaces before reaching the sea. Later protection used a conductive path from a high point to submerged metal, directing current away from vulnerable timber and fittings. The discussion shows why a wooden hull was not automatically insulated from serious lightning damage.

evidence.note: The panel explains exploding wet masts and later conductive paths to the sea.

question: How were Age of Sail ships protected against lightning?
answerShort: Early ships had little deliberate protection, but later conductors carried strikes from the masthead toward the water to reduce destructive paths through the timber.

title: Why combining twin Phalanx guns with RAM is a poor trade
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: A twin-gun Phalanx and RAM mount would be heavy and mechanically complex, while separate missile and gun layers provide a clearer defensive sequence.
body: The proposed mount combines two different interception systems into a very heavy close-in package. Rolling Airframe Missiles should engage threats before they enter the final gun envelope, so sharing one mount offers limited tactical benefit. Twin 20 mm guns add weight and complexity without solving the basic range limitation of Phalanx. If a larger combined installation were justified on a carrier, the panel would prefer a heavier-caliber gun rather than duplicating the existing weapon.

evidence.note: The panel weighs mount weight and complexity, engagement order, and the limited value of duplicating 20 mm guns.

question: Would a twin-barrel Phalanx mount combined with a RAM launcher be useful?
answerShort: Probably not; it would be heavy and complex, and layered defense works better with RAM firing first and a separate gun handling the final engagement.

title: If Dreadnought launched as a Prince of Wales-class battleship
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: A 28-knot, radar-equipped ship with ten 14-inch guns in 1906 would reset the battleship standard and push rival navies toward much larger weapons and hulls.
body: The transformed Dreadnought would combine battlecruiser speed, modern fire control, radar, and a ten-gun 14-inch battery decades ahead of contemporary ships. Existing battleships could not match its range, protection, or ability to control an engagement. Rival navies would treat it as the new baseline and pursue larger 15- and 16-inch designs rather than merely copying historical dreadnoughts. Even the secondary 5.25-inch armament would look like cruiser-caliber firepower in 1906, magnifying the shock to naval planning.

evidence.note: The panel compares speed, ten 14-inch guns, radar, secondary armament, and the likely escalation in foreign designs.

question: What if HMS Dreadnought became a Second World War Prince of Wales-class battleship at launch?
answerShort: It would dominate every contemporary capital ship and provoke a much more extreme arms race in speed, fire control, displacement, and gun caliber.

title: A modern PT boat would be an uncrewed torpedo craft
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: The modern equivalent would be a small, fast, fiberglass uncrewed craft carrying sensors and torpedoes over useful operational range.
body: Rather than recreating a crewed Second World War motor torpedo boat, the answer strips the concept down to the smallest remotely operated hull that can carry weapons and sensors. A fiberglass fast-boat hull offers a practical starting point for low weight and commercial construction. Range matters as much as speed because the craft should threaten movement 1,000 to 2,000 nautical miles from its base. That requirement makes it a reusable uncrewed torpedo platform rather than a disposable explosive jet ski or harbor weapon.

evidence.note: The answer specifies an uncrewed fast craft, fiberglass construction, weapons, sensors, and range.

question: How would you build and power a modern PT boat?
answerShort: Use a small fiberglass fast-boat hull as a long-range uncrewed sensor and torpedo platform.

title: How the Royal Navy sustains a global carrier deployment
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: The carrier group can deploy worldwide, but it still depends on ports and Royal Fleet Auxiliary support rather than nuclear-powered endurance.
body: Queen Elizabeth is conventionally powered, so the comparison with a US nuclear carrier begins from different endurance assumptions. The carrier can remain at sea while Royal Fleet Auxiliary ships enter ports, collect fuel and stores, and shuttle them back to the group. That arrangement gives the force global reach without making it independent of shore infrastructure. The deployment also exposes the consequences of insufficient British investment in auxiliaries and support-ship construction during earlier decades.

evidence.note: The answer explains port use, fleet auxiliaries, and shipbuilding constraints.

question: Can a Royal Navy carrier group operate globally without relying on ports like a US nuclear carrier group?
answerShort: It has global reach but still uses ports indirectly through the Royal Fleet Auxiliary and is constrained by the size of the support fleet.

title: Should the Ship Hector replica return to the water?
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Floating the Hector replica would give visitors a more authentic sense of the ship, but Nova Scotia's climate and the burden of maintaining a wooden hull may favor preservation ashore.
body: The panel welcomes the idea because a ship experienced afloat communicates scale and movement differently from a static exhibit. Public history benefits when visitors can see how a hull sits in the water and imagine the conditions aboard. A wooden replica in Nova Scotia would also face demanding weather, water, and maintenance cycles. The best choice therefore depends on whether the museum can sustain that preservation burden without shortening the vessel's life.

evidence.note: The panel balances the educational value of a floating replica against local climate and preservation costs.

question: Should the replica of the immigrant ship Hector be relaunched after its rebuild?
answerShort: It would improve authenticity and public engagement, but only if the museum can support the much heavier maintenance demands of keeping a wooden replica afloat in Nova Scotia.

title: Would skipping Tulagi improve the Coral Sea ambush?
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Not attacking Tulagi would not materially improve the American carriers' chances; stronger escort air defense would matter more.
body: The panel does not see Fletcher's 4 May strike as the decisive obstacle to surprising the Japanese carriers. Cancelling the Tulagi attack would change some movements and information, but not enough to guarantee a favorable carrier ambush. The larger American weakness was the limited antiaircraft protection available around Yorktown and Lexington. More Atlanta-class cruisers would have had a clearer effect by reducing losses during the early carrier battles.

evidence.note: The answer rejects a major surprise benefit and emphasizes Atlanta-class escorts.

question: If Fletcher had not attacked Tulagi on 4 May 1942, would Yorktown and Lexington have had a better chance of surprising the Japanese at Coral Sea?
answerShort: Probably not; additional air-defense escorts would have improved their prospects more than cancelling the Tulagi strike.

title: Why Harpoon is the wrong missile for a modernized Elco
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: An Elco-sized craft could carry Harpoon in principle, but newer and lighter anti-ship missiles make the old weapon a poor choice.
body: An Elco-sized hull could carry a Harpoon launcher if displacement, stability, and integration were addressed. Physical possibility is not the same as a sensible combat system. Harpoon is mature but aging and imposes more burden than a small craft needs to accept. Naval Strike Missile and newer projects offer better fits for a modern fast-attack craft, so the panel answers yes to feasibility and no to desirability.

evidence.note: The panel distinguishes feasibility from the better modern missile choice.

question: Can a modernized Elco PT boat mount Harpoon?
answerShort: Yes, but it should not: Naval Strike Missile or newer weapons would be more appropriate.

title: Why HMS Acheron's high-pressure steam trial did not spread
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Acheron's experimental high-pressure machinery showed promise, but limited peacetime funding, fragmented industrial responsibility, and the need for sustained development prevented rapid fleet adoption.
body: The panel identifies HMS Acheron as a Royal Navy destroyer used to explore higher-pressure, higher-temperature steam before the Second World War. The installation demonstrated that Britain understood the potential gains in efficiency and power density. Turning a trial plant into a fleet standard required continued work across boiler builders, turbine firms, yards, and naval engineering authorities. Tight interwar budgets and inconsistent institutional sponsorship interrupted that progression, leaving later programs to recover knowledge rather than inherit a mature 700-psi system.

evidence.note: The panel discusses Acheron's experimental plant, the intended pressure progression, British boiler and turbine firms, funding, and institutional continuity.

question: Why did the Royal Navy not continue from HMS Acheron's high-pressure steam experiment?
answerShort: The technology needed sustained, coordinated development, but peacetime funding and fragmented industrial leadership prevented the trial from becoming a fleet program.

title: From early experiments to Chicago Pile-1
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Early experiments could control fission without producing useful power, while Chicago Pile-1 achieved the first self-sustaining chain reaction in 1942.
body: The panel distinguishes a device that demonstrates or controls fission from a reactor capable of sustaining a chain reaction. Earlier laboratory arrangements could reveal nuclear behavior without producing useful heat or power. Chicago Pile-1 achieved the first self-sustaining nuclear chain reaction in 1942 under Enrico Fermi's team. That definition makes 1942 the practical milestone while explaining why someone using a looser meaning of working reactor might propose an earlier date.

evidence.note: The panel separates early fission experiments from Chicago Pile-1's self-sustaining reaction.

question: What is the earliest date at which a working nuclear reactor could be built?
answerShort: Primitive experiments were possible earlier, but the first self-sustaining reactor was Chicago Pile-1 in 1942.

title: The leading destroyer-escort candidates
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: The panel's shortlist centers on the Allen M. Sumner class and Britain's Batch III Hunt class, judged against the escort needs of their period.
body: The answer is framed relative to each design's period and escort mission rather than as a contest detached from operational purpose. The transcript's repeated 'Sumners' identifies the Allen M. Sumner class as the American candidate. The panel favors it while also identifying the Batch III Hunt class as a strong British alternative. The exchange is brief, so it offers a shortlist rather than a complete design comparison.

evidence.note: The panel names the Sumners and Batch III Hunts as its leading candidates.

question: What was the ultimate destroyer escort?
answerShort: The panel favors the Allen M. Sumner class, with the Batch III Hunt class as the other leading candidate.

title: An 800-psi machinery plant for a Nelson successor
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Higher-pressure machinery could support an F3-like 35,000-ton ship approaching 28 knots, though the twin-shaft design details remain an open research problem.
body: The proposed redesign uses the F3 hull form and devotes the machinery gain largely to speed rather than radically changing the armament. A 35,000-ton ship could aim for roughly 28 knots while retaining the broad Nelson-era battleship concept. F2 and F3 studies show that British designers expected that performance from a twin-shaft arrangement. The unresolved research question is how they intended to transmit the required power through those shafts and propellers, not whether the design studies specified the speed.

evidence.note: The answer names the F3 form, displacement, speed, and twin-shaft issue.

question: What features would you add when redesigning Nelson with 800-psi machinery?
answerShort: Use an F3-like form and aim for about 28 knots within 35,000 tons, while retaining the broad battleship concept.

title: Why the Velox boiler did not become a fleet standard
videoTitle: Bruships 202: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: Velox machinery offered theoretical high output but imposed complexity and poor shipboard volume efficiency, so Royal Navy trials did not justify wider adoption.
body: The Royal Navy did test the Velox boiler rather than dismissing it without evidence. Its forced-flow arrangement promised high output, but the complete installation was mechanically complicated. Once auxiliaries and the space needed to operate and maintain the system were counted, its practical power density was less attractive than the headline figures suggested. The trial therefore served its purpose by showing that a theoretically powerful boiler was not necessarily the best fleet engineering solution.

evidence.note: The panel describes Royal Navy testing, the boiler's complexity, and its disappointing practical power density and space use.

question: Why did the Royal Navy not adopt the Velox boiler after testing it?
answerShort: The complete system was too complex and space-inefficient aboard ship for its theoretical output to justify fleet-wide adoption.

title: Why historical knowledge remains a balance of probabilities
videoTitle: Bruships 234: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: An opening methodological aside explains why even eyewitness evidence cannot make a historical reconstruction completely certain.
body: Historical accounts are assembled from evidence that is incomplete and shaped by each observer's position. Even someone present at an event sees only one perspective, so direct observation does not remove every uncertainty. The responsible approach is to weigh the available sources and express conclusions as a balance of probabilities rather than claiming absolute knowledge.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke rejects the idea that historians should speak only when they know something with complete certainty, noting that even eyewitnesses have a limited perspective and that history works through balances of probability.

title: Mahan and Corbett emphasize different parts of sea power
videoTitle: Bruships 234: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: An opening discussion argues that Mahan and Corbett share more strategic ground than a simple battle-versus-commerce contrast suggests.
body: Dr. Clarke presents both theorists as advocates of sea power who wrote for different audiences and therefore stressed different problems. Mahan had to establish the need for American sea power, while Corbett could focus more directly on how an already maritime British state should use it; reading beyond a few famous chapters reveals their overlap on convoys, communications, and national strategy.

evidence.note: The panel compares Mahan and Corbett, their audiences, their common concern with sea power, and British national identification with naval power.

title: Why an angled flight deck and well deck conflict
videoTitle: Bruships 234: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: A hybrid carrier-amphibious design is technically possible, but docking-down for well-deck operations undermines conventional fixed-wing flight operations.
body: Flooding a well deck slows the ship and changes its trim, while an angled flight deck is intended for aircraft that need a stable deck moving at useful speed into the wind. Helicopters and STOVL aircraft tolerate the compromise better, which is why paper designs can combine both features even though an effective fleet carrier generally should not.

evidence.note: The answer explains the speed and trim consequences of well-deck operation and why these conflict with conventional carrier aviation.

question: Can a ship combine an angled flight deck with a well deck, effectively turning a Wasp-class assault ship into a fleet carrier?
answerShort: It is technically possible, but docking-down for well-deck use makes conventional carrier operations impractical; helicopters and STOVL aircraft fit the compromise better.

title: Defending stationary warships at Bahrain
videoTitle: Bruships 234: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: A question about British ships at Bahrain considers why a moored frigate or mine-countermeasures vessel is not simply able to fight off an incoming attack.
body: Dr. Clarke stresses that a ship alongside is a stationary target and may not be configured to employ its full sensors or missile armament. He also points to the wider base defences and the attacker's targeting accuracy, rather than treating the ships as independently invulnerable.

evidence.note: The answer discusses stationary British ships at the Bahrain support facility, their likely readiness state, and surrounding defences.

question: How could British warships alongside at Bahrain avoid an Iranian attack when they were stationary targets?
answerShort: A moored ship may not be configured to use all of its own weapons; base defences and the attacker's targeting also matter.

title: Gulf energy routes make naval absence consequential
videoTitle: Bruships 234: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: A discussion of British policy in the Gulf connects naval presence to liquefied-natural-gas routes, energy prices, and the political cost of having little control over a maritime crisis.
body: Dr. Clarke points from Britain's lack of an active warship in the Gulf to the route taken by Qatari liquefied natural gas and the likely effect of conflict on British electricity and gas prices. The passage shows why naval presence is not only about joining strikes: ships, bases, and access can provide options around a strategic trade corridor, while absence leaves a government exposed to economic consequences without much influence over events.

evidence.note: The discussion links the absence of an active British warship in the Gulf with Qatari LNG routes, higher domestic energy costs, base access, and limited political influence over the crisis.

title: Why major naval disasters rarely exclude politics
videoTitle: Bruships 234: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: A search for the worst event imposed on a navy without its own politicians' responsibility shows how strategy, readiness, command appointments, and rules of engagement usually connect major disasters to political choices.
body: The panel tests Copenhagen, the Spanish Armada, Tsushima, the Medway, Coronel, the Falkland Islands, River Plate, and Bismarck against the question. Most failures lead back to funding, basing, appointments, missions, or rules set by governments, leaving only narrower operational decisions as plausible exceptions.

evidence.note: The panel compares a series of naval disasters and repeatedly identifies political decisions behind fleet posture, command, and mission.

question: What is the worst thing that happened to a navy without being caused by its own politicians?
answerShort: Almost every fleet-scale disaster can be traced partly to political decisions; narrower cases such as the Falkland Islands or River Plate come closest to a primarily naval failure.

title: When aircraft gas turbines gain naval cousins
videoTitle: Bruships 234: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: A discussion of A380 engines explains why successful aerospace turbine families often have related marine variants rather than becoming one-for-one ship engines.
body: The panel identifies existing naval derivatives associated with the Trent and GE engine families and notes that manufacturers try to reuse a proven design across markets. The exchange also distinguishes a shared design lineage from the different installations and maintenance demands of aviation and warship propulsion.

evidence.note: The exchange compares the A380's engine families with existing naval variants and discusses turbine design lineage.

question: Could the gas turbines used on an Airbus A380 be adapted for naval propulsion?
answerShort: Related engine families already have marine derivatives; manufacturers commonly reuse a successful core design in naval applications.

title: Why destroyer captains were not routinely given stimulants
videoTitle: Bruships 234: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: The wartime use of amphetamines in aircraft and ground forces did not translate neatly to destroyer command, where sustained judgment mattered more than short bursts of reaction speed.
body: A warship captain could hand over the watch and rest outside immediate combat, while the command role demanded emotional control and careful decisions over long periods. The medical discussion adds that stimulants can postpone sleep but also impair judgment, create dependence, and produce an unpredictable crash.

evidence.note: The exchange contrasts naval command requirements with tanker and pilot stimulant use and discusses sleep, dependence, and judgment.

question: Did navies try giving amphetamines to destroyer captains as armies and air forces did with some crews?
answerShort: Not as a normal practice: destroyer command relied on sustained judgment and watch relief, while stimulants risked impaired decisions, dependence, and an eventual crash.

title: Spanish ships remembered for sail training and mine warfare
videoTitle: Bruships 234: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: The panel's favorite twentieth-century Spanish ships range from the sail-training ship Juan Sebastián de Elcano to the Segura-class minehunters.
body: The exchange highlights two very different forms of naval value: a handsome training vessel that represents maritime tradition and modern minehunters designed for a specialized operational task. Other panelists note that limited familiarity with Spanish warships constrains their choices rather than forcing a confident ranking.

evidence.note: Panelists select the Spanish sail-training ship and Segura-class minehunters and state the limits of their familiarity.

question: What is the panel's favorite Spanish ship of the twentieth century?
answerShort: The main choices are Juan Sebastián de Elcano and the Segura-class minehunters, with the panel openly noting where its knowledge is limited.

title: A cautious estimate for Nagato's barbette width
videoTitle: Bruships 234: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
